How I healed my “inner child” (an Exiled Part, in Internal Family Systems terms) in psychedelic and holotropic states of consciousness, with the power of loving my children. Get some tissues and buckle up.

Some background: white dude in my early 40s, married 17 years. My 7 yo son is A Little Too Much Like Me, and my daughter is 2. Talk therapy for a decade, meditated for 7, got Very Serious about inner work in nonordinary states of consciousness over the last ~2 years.

My parents met when my dad was on a manic bender with his then-undiagnosed bipolar. Fun guy. So they got married two weeks later. Two years and a few psych wards later, she was going to leave him. Had gall bladder surgery, forgot her birth control, and I was conceived.

So they stayed together to raise a family, my kid brother entered the chat two years later. Dad was levelled out by lithium by this point but deeply resentful of never getting to be manic again. He was a shit dad and husband.

My mom drilled this story into me over the years to convince me of her love. She suffered through their marriage for me! It gave me guilt and shame that I was responsible for this. As far back as I can remember, I thought that it would be better if I didn’t exist.

Fast forward to a high dose mushroom journey, where I relived my birth (shoutout Stan Grof’s perinatal theory). Rage as the walls closed in, followed by a sense that my anger was not welcome here, that this environment was unsafe and I must be careful with it.

My mom in the vision was not happy to see me like she’s always told me (did I mention she’s an inveterate liar?). It’s not that she didn’t also love me, she did, but that earliest sense was that I was unwanted, unwelcome.

I’ve also done a lot of self- (or Self-)directed work in the Internal Family Systems (IFS) paradigm over the last year or so. It’s a very helpful framework for dealing with things that come up in psychedelic or holotropic breathwork journeys.

Now we’re getting to the good part! I was in a breathwork session and came to that inner space where I can have a converse, Self and Parts. I’d recently met a character I call Sid the Dissociator, who numbs me out whenever things get too intense. (A Firefighter in IFS terms.)

In IFS, Protectors like Sid are trying to prevent an Exile from getting triggered. Manager-type protectors (like an inner critic) try to prevent anything from happening that will stir up the strong feelings in some Exile, usually attached to basic emotions like fear or anger.

Safety interlude: do not go to an Exile without guidance from an IFS therapist and permission from your Protectors (which the therapist will get). Your Protectors will go on high alert and proceed to trash your life. (I got permission, but did this work solo, please be careful.)

So I’m talking to Sid, asking him what’s so bad inside me that he has to numb me out all the time (weed, porn, booze, watching sports, mindlessly scrolling social media, whatever it takes). Sid is an angry character, and he said, “fine, you want to see?” and stepped aside.

Then I was holding a baby in my arms, the same baby I had been in that perinatal experience. I knew that this Exile Part was my sense of Rejection, of unwelcome, and I called it Reggie. And Reggie was horrifically beaten and bloody and raw. Wailing, alone, uncared for.

Normally in Parts work, you can talk to them. But Exiles are usually manifestations of much younger periods in life. If you talk to them, they usually think you’re much younger. But Reggie was too young for words, miserable and inconsolable to boot. I didn’t know what to do.

I was overwhelmed. Reggie had been lying on the floor, screaming for the whole of my life. This is the pain at the heart of me. “Don’t you see why I do what I have to!” screamed Sid from behind me. “I keep him safe! Nobody cares!”

I could not speak to Reggie, I could not communicate to him how much this was just an accident of birth, this deep-seated rejection. How much I am actually loved by my family and good friends, accepted. I know that they are glad that I’m here but Reggie, Reggie is inconsolable.

But I’m a good dad. I know how to hold a baby with tenderness and warmth and love and welcome, like I have with my children. I let all of that flood into this flayed child at the heart of me, no words needed because I am Reggie. Tears flowed, then, and tears are flowing now.

I healed my inner child with the love I have for my children. Reggie now knew that his experience was not universal, that there is love, because I have loved my kids. Incredibly imperfectly, but I have loved them.

I don’t feel like I don’t belong on this earth any more. My life is not ordered around avoiding the pain of rejection any more. Habitually, yes, because that’s what I’ve always done. But energetically, no, because there’s no separate, isolated Reggie left to protect.
I belong here, and you do too. And I’m here to help. I love you.

Read this thread. SSRIs can and do help, but the "chemical imbalance" theory is woefully inadequate and leads to systemic harm.

“Here, take these drugs and call me never.”

It's like giving people an opiate prescpition for their pain and never suturing the obvious gaping wound. https://t.co/Qn9TO5PVDS

Not only this, but SSRIs come with downsides that are rarely adequately disclosed. They:

1. stop working over time
2. have (often horrible) side effects
3. are addictive. Read firsthand accounts of SSRI tapering and compare to opiod withdrawal.

And if you haven’t used the limited window of SSRI helpfulness to do some deep inner healing, you’re fucked. Go off this SSRI, deal with withdrawal symptoms that were worse than the disease, and for what? Just to try a different SSRI that might not do jack shit.

And a HUGE problem is that SSRIs blunt psychedelic efficacy. My dear friend with crippling depression wants to try MDMA trauma therapy (after much drug war deprogramming). Only trouble is: she’s on an SSRI and will have to go through tapering hell to even try.

We are much more than chemical machines. We are at least bio-psycho-social-spiritual beings, and perhaps much more. People with mental health problems are responding to crises in some or all of these systems. We need more than symptomatic blunting, we need healing at every level.

A protocol for healing body dysmorphia with MDMA in a solo setting.

Or: some shit I did and it was profoundly healing and might help you, who knows. n=1 and all that. 🧵
1. You *must* be alone. This is powerful stuff and being interrupted or witnessed will almost definitely go poorly. This is about *your* relationship to *your* body.
2. Other needs: a full length mirror and some music you enjoy dancing to (probably leaning towards happier/brighter vibes). There should be enough light to clearly see yourself in the mirror.

3. Take a “full” dose of MDMA that has been tested for purity or is otherwise trustworthy. (Alternately try a research chemical analogue, such as the “Borax Combo,” after doing due diligence.) You want a full strength dose, whatever that is for you.

4. Spend your time coming up wholesomely. I suggest meditating, or walking, or taking a shower. Something soothing and pleasant. Set your intention that you will learn to be happy to inhabit the body you’re in.

5. When you’re ready to get up and move, do it. And here’s where it gets a bit weird: get stark naked, because you’re going to be dancing in front of that  full length mirror for the rest of your trip.

6. Look at your unclothed body in the mirror and love every part of it. Everything; love, Love, LOVE. See how it’s kept you alive, all this time. See how it is good, and strong, and here. It is you. You are it.
7. Dissolve every self-hating, self-critical story about your body in the waves of love and connection that MDMA helps to bring. You are good. Your body is good. Every last part of you.
8. What other body are you supposed to have? You already have this one, the only one of its kind in the world. It radiates beauty, it is perfect. Dissolve all lies to the contrary with a loving gaze.
9. Keep going for as long as you can. The MDMA should help with both energy and with loving that reflected body. I danced in this way for ~5 hours.
10. Stay hydrated! Have good integration practices afterwards! (My current personal favourite is audio journaling while going for a walk.)
11. Theoretical notes: this is some kind of combo of exposure therapy, mirror box therapy, and metta (lovingkindness).

12. Personal notes: the idea for this arose spontaneously in a recent solo MDMA session, my first time using it in 22 years (I was a raver). I wanted to do inner work but I also wanted to dance. Inspiration struck, why not both?

13. I have noticed many results since the session. I have always felt vaguely stuck in terms of body image in my 20s (I’m 43). I feel my age, now, and great about it. I look at other men my age and no longer see them as “my dad’s age.”
14. I just like being a cishet male now. Lots of religious shame building on shame from my mother because she had shitty men in her life. Men are bad, I am bad. Not any more! Lock up your mothers!
15. Although I’ve always been firmly hetero, I explored the possibility of bisexuality for the first time ever. It’s moved from a hard “no” to “maybe with the right guy, but pretty unsure.”
16. If you try this, let me know how it went! If you want to try this and have questions, ask me!

@puheenix Exposure therapy is a way to retrain your body’s reaction to certain stimuli that you’ve perceived as threatening, difficult, bad, etc. @VividVoid_'s interview with @tasshinfogleman  describes his own process with it: https://t.co/Um53MCVuTG

@puheenix @VividVoid_ @tasshinfogleman MDMA therapy for PTSD makes is a kind of exposure therapy along with memory reconsolidation to rewire our perception of memories that we have perceived as threatening/traumatic/aversive. More on this: https://t.co/YUz9lPwY5l

@puheenix @VividVoid_ @tasshinfogleman Mirror box therapy entrains the body’s mirror neurons to perceive a reflection as one’s own body, used very effectively among amputees to heal from phantom limb pain. If it can be used for that, I might be able to use it to perceive my body as my own, and to love it.

@puheenix @VividVoid_ @tasshinfogleman Think about our normal modes of looking in a mirror: what’s wrong with this face? How can I/should I change it? If we catch a glimpse of ourseleves in the mirror, especially naked, how often do we look away in disgust? This habituates us to dissociate from the reflected body.

@fortymost Oh hell yeah that's a great topic. Definitely a huge area that I'll have to dig into more over time but let me give some off-the-cuff thoughts.

@fortymost First, with MDMA, you simply can’t use it very often. It’s neurotoxic and overuse can damage your ability to get much of anything out of it again, ever. Those in the MAPS MDMA trials typically have 3 sessions, never more than 3 weeks apart, typically 4-6 weeks.
@fortymost People who use MDMA without authorization have generally settled on no more than once a month, with many advocating for a 3 month minimum between use. Less is definitely more with MDMA.

@fortymost I will be interested to see what comes of the benzofuran class of MDMA analogues, like 5-MAPB and 6-APB. They were developed by the legend David Nichols specifically to be less neurotoxic than MDMA. I eagerly await future studies on these but all the focus is on MDMA currently.

@fortymost There are some things you can do to reduce MDMA neurotoxicity as well: supplementation, avoiding overheated environments and too much physical activity (sorry ravers), and avoiding redosing, however tempting. More on supplementation: https://t.co/clT8ltzYmi

@fortymost Classic psychedelics of like mushrooms and LSD do not present neurotoxicity dangers, but you need at least 3-5 days to recover tolerance from high doses and you shouldn’t be taking high doses that often anyway because nobody can integrate that. Too destabilizing.

@fortymost Which brings me to integration: the most basic rule of thumb is that you don’t go into a high dose trip without having integrated the lessons of the previous one. It should feel settled into your being, not something that you’re continuing to work through.

@fortymost Good integration means you should have some kind of ongoing spiritual practice, and the more the merrier. Journaling, meditation, yoga, martial arts, imaginal practices like IFS, talk therapy, prayer, service, whatever connects you to yourself, your community, and the Ultimate.
@fortymost Other stray thoughts: long-term sustainable use will likely include taking extended breaks from substances. The whole point is to live better in the world, not to escape from it.

@fortymost And don't overlook the utility of low (not micro) doses as an adjunct to inner work. Some of my most powerful transformative experiences have come from .5g of mushrooms to do IFS work.

Ego death is not the only valuable psychedelic experience.

What I hate about scientific psychedelic trials (aside from the abuse and gaslighting!) is the unchangeable control of method, using a ~60 year old psychotherapeutic model ignorant of current trauma research.

The PSIP model heals trauma, with cannabis(!), ketamine, or MDMA.🧵

PSIP (Psychedelic Somatic Interactional Psychology) was developed by former MDMA trial therapist Saj Razvi. Some MDMA recipients experienced no classic symptoms of love, empathy, connection. They’d feel nothing, for most or all of a session.

Nothing.

But Saj knew that PTSD shows up in both panic/rage attacks AND disscation. Dissociation is key: the body stuck in dissociation is producing massive quantities of opiods, which are routinely used as (drumroll) trip killers!

What to do about a body stuck in dissociation?

Ok theory time, I’ll be as brief as possible (hard, I ❤️ theorygeeking).

Humans are mammals. All mammals share a similar ANS (autonomic nervous system), including a fight-flight-freeze system, which activates in times of extreme stress, danger, or suffering.

But humans also have a “higher”/newer brain capable of  ordered, time-based thinking. It can override some select functions of the ANS like breathing deliberately, and (drumroll) the repression of sensations/signals from the body.

The traumatic energy gets stuck. This is PTSD.

How does the ANS discharge traumatic energy?

Imagine we’re a zebra with our herd. We hear a rustling bush. Potential threat. Ears perk, vision enhanced. But it’s just the wind, the zebra relaxes back down to what PSIP calls State 0. Polyvagal theory calls this “safe and social.”

The grass rustles again. State 1 activation, like before, ears perk, elevated heartrate, and yes, there’s a lion in the bushes. State 1 sticks, we can stay elevated like this for a long time (a lifetime). Vigilant. Looking for threat. https://t.co/CjZDLmZStZ

The lion explodes into a run after you. Run for your zebra life! State 2 activates, pumping adrenaline like there’s no tomorrow - there might not be! Everything is fast, you’re panicked, oh god I want to live… https://t.co/Avu70mbUmv

But you, the zebra, did not get away from the lion. As its jaws sink around your neck, your body, overwhelmed by threat, releases some of its own opiates. Everything slows down, numbs, collapses, goes cold. 

This is State 3. https://t.co/gmnDOIrBNx

State 3 still clings to a sliver of hope. Mostly dead, but desiring escape. Maybe hyenas show up, the lion’s distracted, you arise all at once, run run! State 2 again.

Or, if not, the lion begins to dine, and we get a State 4 *flood* of internal opiates. Fade to black. https://t.co/kOuAkX7TSn

Back to the zebra getaway: State 2 reactivates, the zebra runs for its life. The lion isn’t chasing, you gain distance, drop into State 1, still vigilant, but no longer under imminent threat. Heartrate receding, not all the way, not until we’re safe.

Mammals are only safe in the herd. The zebra returns, touches its kin. It may shake, quiver, roll, vocalize. Discharging all that pent-up ANS energy. More touching, more shaking, until the close contact with the herd allows the restoration of a safe and social state: State 0.

But if humans don't feel safe in our herd (our families and communities) we can’t and won’t get back to 0. We get PTSD: undischarged ANS energy looping and retriggering as we confuse these stuck energies for threat itself.

Ongoing threat and abuse compounds this.

People can live their whole lives cycling between State 1/2 activation or 3/4 dissociation, although State 2 (👋🏻 panic attacks) can’t be sustained.

We panic, we numb. Use uppers and downers to help us cycle more skillfully, coping with our activation symptoms.

Now we get back to Saj, and dissociation, or States 3/4 as we now know them. State 3 is associated with depression, but State 4 is total unfeeling numbness, equivalent to ~4x the amount of morphine given for breakthrough pain in the hospital.

Psychedelics have no effect here.

Saj started using ketamine, and cannabis, as better tools for helping to crack dissociation, and a technique called Selective Inhibition (SI) to go with it. 

SI directs you to feel your body with no copes. No deep breaths, no subtle movement, just feel the body and go with it.

Why cannabis or ketamine? They repress executive function to help us allow our VERY uncomfortable ANS processes to manifest so we can get back to 0.

Embarrassing shit like shaking and crying that we'd normally repress. But you’re a mammal, and you need this to get to zero.

The ANS discharges in reverse: from 4 to 3 to 2 to 1 to 0; 3 to 2 being most dramatic. The body will shake, mime running, cry out. Depression suddenly transforms into a desperation to live!

Several such waves may be carried out in a single session, lasting around 2hrs.

And who knows if this will work for everyone, but holy shit did it work for me (in a self-guided cannabis session). One 1.5hr session = the single biggest change in my baseline wellbeing.

My whole personality structure of coping with that activation, blessedly obsolete.

And hey now that dissociation is cracked, you can work with psychedelics SO much more productively! ANS trauma discharge with SI is merely one part of Tier 1 in the PSIP model, “Body” in the attached graphic. https://t.co/1xEmY3uXH7

Tiers 2 and 3 are where the biographical and transpersonal/mystical stuff can really flow, now that our ANS isn’t activating inappropriately (bad trips thanks to State 1/2) and/or blocking psychedelic activity (State 3/4 dissociation).

You were stuck, not broken.

What I love about PSIP is that it’s a skillful model, not focused on any particular medicine, but rather on outcomes, sensitively-applied technique, and trauma research. It makes sense of why the old "hands-off" model of psychedelic tripping does not work for everyone.

Unfortunately, the politics of psychedelic research means that only drug efficacy is being studied. We have to control for technique (and don’t even get started on blinding) or we’re Not Doing Science. If you don’t get better, you’re an obstruction to Moving Forward.

But you don’t have to wait for lawmakers or scientists to give you permission to heal. Your body already knows how, and Saj's PSIP model can help you to let it.

Learn to go solo like me, or find a PSIP trained therapist: https://t.co/ctFG5L1EsO

If neither of those work for you, DM me and I’ll try my best to help. I’m unauthorized and in no way affiliated with PSIP or Saj, but I’m not unskilled. I love you, and you might too, if you can get to zero. Then the fun (life!) begins.

A brief meditation on (re-)entering creative flow states

A piece of writing advice I've seen a few times is to quit a writing session in the middle of a good flow. A paragraph, or even sentence, unfinished. The idea is that you'll fall back into flow to finish it, next time.

Working theory: just as mastery of the jhanas means that you can simply incline to a remembrance of that state of bliss (or any feeling), maybe you can also recall, strengthen, and bank like a coal, fanned into flame, that state of mind known as creative flow.

What if my mind were simply developed to the point where I could incline towards “creative writing flow” and boom, there I was? To recall moments of past flow, to sense how that felt in the body, heart, and mind, and activating that and entering it, now?

I'm beginning to roughly distinguish two modes of psychedelic intervention into mental/spiritual health: 1. surgery and 2. prevention (using a physical health analogy).

1. Surgery: high dose, ego death, dramatic interventions. Risky, but worthwhile. The greater the power, the greater the risk. Work can be done here that can't be done in any other way, or at least not nearly as quickly. 

But constant surgery is not good for you.

2. Prevention: low dose (psycholitic) practices that nurture/develop ongoing mental health and spiritual growth. Most attention is on the more dramatic “surgical” interventions. 

Your ego might eventually die permanently, but in the meantime, make it healthier and happier.

These observations have been borne of the insight that a lot of people (at one time myself included) pursue ego death because they hate themselves.

Ego death is absolutely a release, and a relief, for someone stuck in self-loathing. But once you come down, the work begins.

@IntoTheBlues Ahhh very cool. I think we can go about a lot of things with more gentleness and care than we tend to do. And even the most extreme interventions can be eased into (titrated) a bit more.

A small story to illustrate worst-case scenario thinking.

I was at a company retreat in Utah, which has some strange alcohol laws which meant that the ballroom we were using couldn't serve us booze most of the time. But, we had a couple of social suites with well-stocked bars. https://t.co/AxJ8b0JeQg
So I would fill my bag with drinks and offer them to people in the ballroom. Most people accepted gladly, but some would recoil, with some form of fear about getting in trouble.

@haarball_ I follow Rene Girard when it comes to explanations of human culture and violence. He argues that human sacrifice stands at the heart of the whole thing, because we're mimetic (imitative) and can easily get into negative feedback loops where we overwhelm each other's systems.

@haarball_ Human sacrifice is the imperfect solution to these overwhelms. Over time we figure out less violent means (eg ritual) of relieving some of that pressure that builds up. Sports is a late iteration along those lines, projecting a fictionalized participation in ritualized violence.

A scatological reflection.

I just took a big beautiful dump and had nobody to show it to. When my brother and I lived together, we would often show off our epic shits to each other, bursting with pride and mirth.

It got me thinking about bodies: our ability to take in all that we’ve ingested, break it down into what’s beneficial, and pass on that which we can’t make use of. This is a metaphor for spiritual discernment, but also for processing trauma.

In trauma, we experienced something truly indigestible, and it’s been stuck there, for months or years or a lifetime. Every move we make is filled with discomfort. Trauma is like constipation. We can never digest anything on its own without reference to that stuckness.

There are many biopsychospiritual tools for giving us what amounts to a spiritual enema, clearing out stuck trauma and blocked energy so that our system can function normally once again, taking in the good, and shitting out the rest.

The collective does not emerge from individuals. Rather, the individual emerges from the collective.

How to do Drugs for Healing and Spiritual Growth While Your Kids are at School https://t.co/Ro2GksNVeR

@tasshinfogleman I had no way to participate in any of the existing psychedelics models thanks to prohibition and parenting. So I invented my own path with the time I had available, which was when my kids were at school. Take a sick day, have a trip day.

@tasshinfogleman As I recently told a new friend, over the past year I have aggressively treated my oft-crippling anxiety and depression that sat on top of undiagnosed cPTSD with a self-designed, ever-evolving protocol of therapies, many of them psychedelic-assisted.

@tasshinfogleman Nearly all of these explorations were done solo, in my home. There is very little guidance out there for solo practitioners such as myself, and much less for those with parental responsibilities that cannot get away for a full weekend of preparation, dosing, and integration.

@tasshinfogleman I share what I’ve learned, so far, about tripping solo on tight timelines. It’s been an absolute pleasure to see narratives and habits and coping strategies that once seemed hardwired into me, melt and morph and transform. What has been programmed, can be reprogrammed.
@tasshinfogleman Here’s the article: https://t.co/U5N9592tQR

@tasshinfogleman And, as a bonus, I am now open to working with people in pursuit of their own healing, with a focus on trauma. Wiio’s Laws:

1. Communication usually fails, except by accident.
  1.1 If communication can fail, it will.
  1.2 If communication cannot fail, it still most usually fails.
  1.3 If communication seems to succeed in the intended way, there's a misunderstanding.

1.4 If you are content with your message, communication certainly fails.

2. If a message can be interpreted in several ways, it will be interpreted in a manner that maximizes the damage.
3. There is always someone who knows better than you what you meant with your message.

4. The more we communicate, the worse communication succeeds.
  4.1 The more we communicate, the faster misunderstandings propagate.
5. In mass communication, the important thing is not how things are but how they seem to be.
6. The importance of a news item is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
7. The more important the situation is, the more probable you had forgotten an essential thing that you remembered a moment ago.

I find it endlessly fascinating to watch the reception of a novel psychedelic in realtime on a web forum, from 2004  → as recently as six months ago. Hell, why don't I summarize as I go here.

5-MeO-DALT was first synthesized by Sasha Shulgin around 2004.

Somebody got their hands on a copy of his notes/synthesis instructions and it wound up online and quickly into every research chemical shop because hey, new Shulgin just dropped.
As a harm-reduction move, since 5-MeO-DALT was already making a stir in the grey market, Shulgin published his TiHKAL-style entry for the substance on Erowid. https://t.co/auTpRGYC4E

In the end it never caught on because it wasn't that powerful and mostly lacked visuals. Quality/purity issues abounded and some people clearly had received the wrong chemical. Eyeballing doses. ER visits. Just chaotic frontline fuck prohibition science.

But I'm reading through this and I see 5-MeO-DALT as a substance that's RIPE for therapeutic and growth-oriented exploration.

1. reports of empathogenic qualities
2. short durations (5-30 mins vaporized, 2-3 hrs oral)
3. no/little visual hallucinations (distracting)
[cont'd]

4. generally not much anxiety
5. mostly clearheaded
6. primary effects seem "energy body"-oriented
7. gentle comedown

Lots of ongoing comparisons to 5-MeO-DMT with the vaporized route. Seems like it goes somewhere similar, but with less intensity and lower propensity to anxiety. All of this sounds very compelling for potential trauma therapy.

The earliest therapists working with MDMA (like Friederike Meckel Fischer in Germany and Leo Zeff in the USA) did a lot of group practice from the start because it was clearly more powerful. 

And practically, it scales the reach of the therapist and drives costs down. https://t.co/zp4Lon9Atq

People would be initiated by the therapist individually, and if things went ok they might be invited to the group sessions, where 10-20 people would trip over a weekend (arrive Friday, dose Saturday, group sharing and integration on Sunday).

Everybody attested to the greater power of the group sessions and many became dear friends to one another and so this is a powerful way to form communitas, which is itself predictive of well being. 

We are made of, and for, each other.

Of course, group practice has long been fundamental to indigenous entheogenic rituals. Individuals dosing would have been unthinkable, except occasionally a shamanic figure going on vision quests, but always on behalf of the community, mediating its relations with the unseen.

@SteveRio Completely agree. The particularly interesting thing for me is in the much lower dose range, which I think has been under-explored. Making your own allows you to much more carefully control the dose, which is critical for working with such powerful medicine.

Three Rules of Parenting

These are not morals, they are insights into the normal rhythms and cycles of life with children. My wife and I developed these for sanity and enjoyment.

1. If ain't broke, don't fix it
2. This will end in tears
3. Get on the floor

1. If it ain't broke, don't fix it

New parents stress when their baby is calm or sleeping, wondering if there's something else they should be doing. If the kid isn't crying, there's nothing to do but 1) enjoy! or 2) get some rest, because this state is precious, and fleeting.

2. This will end in tears

Rule 1 has a shelf life. Children cry a LOT and it's not a sign of failure. You rested and enjoyed during Rule 1 so that you can better respond when Rule 2 inevitably appears. This is normal stuff. We laugh and shrug: "Rule 2 strikes again!"

3. Get on the floor

You are a giant to your child. Getting on the floor makes you far more accessible to them. Our bodies are built for touch and contact with each other and our children haven't yet been socialized into pretending at self-sufficiency.

Rule 3 is also great when you're exhausted (you will be). Just lie there and they'll crawl all over you and play around you. Just be there and that's generally enough. Being there enables Rule 1, and you're close by when Rule 2 strikes.

Of course these dynamics play out in the rest of life, too. Cycles of joy and sorrow are in every relationship. The ever-present invitation to not miss what's right here, right now;  showing up to your life and the people in it.
Most parenting advice is selling you something by making you first feel bad about yourself. Fuck that shit. Enjoy your one, wild, life.

@danielbrottman Hmm happy to.

Lot of this in Charles Taylor's "A Secular Age." But the real meat of it, for me, comes from Rene Girard, who engages extensively with Nietzsche while refuting him convincingly. (He does so in a way that also deeply affectionate). eg https://t.co/zNYToK7m3g

@danielbrottman Girard also calls Christianity the religion that ends religion, because most religions (including actually existing Christianity) are founded on violence, however hidden. The Gospels are about unveiling violence, showing that the scapegoat is innocent, that we are the angry gods.

@danielbrottman Highly highly recommend tracking down David Cayley's CBC Idea's 5 part "The Scapegoat" series if you want to dig into Girard, it's excellent. Here's a nice YT link of the whole thing https://t.co/QIXTMIhnSO but you should be able to find it in Cayley's own podcast too

@shorezero thanks for asking! i have found no theory as convincing as that of rene girard. his “the scapegoat” is a great introduction. his mimetic theory has broad and deep explanatory power.

@shorezero an attempt: human desire is contagious, copied, mimetic: we suggest desirability of objects to each other. when the object cannot be shared (eg love triangles), rivalry easily escalates into violence and spreads to the whole community.

@shorezero this builds and builds until the community finds someone, anyone, to blame for all this, a scapegoat. the community is once again united through this, and institutes sacrifice as a universal problem solver (ritual starts here).

@shorezero but why did we stop? girard’s answer is shocking: because the xn gospels, for the first time in history, tell a story about how the sacrificial victim is totally innocent, and this has pervaded culture to the point where we believe victims at least as often as persecutors.
@actualjoe turns out that a religious movement forged in the underclass is not well suited to those who want to assume that god blesses the way they rule the world

@AskYatharth @QiaochuYuan @meekaale eg for me, as a 5, I start with "knowing shit." knowing shit is cool, but a cope, too. it can contract as readily as expand, becoming Smaug dissociated in his cave with all his treasure (knowledge).

@AskYatharth @QiaochuYuan @meekaale But the integration arrow for 5s is 8. That means that knowledge must lead towards power, towards engagement, towards communion with the world. But again that's only the first two notes of the chord, let's keep going.

@AskYatharth @QiaochuYuan @meekaale 8 integrates to 2. This takes all the BDE of the 8 and prevents it from becoming a self-fixated tyrant only interested in service to self. The 2 energy is archetypally about service to others, self-giving, kenosis. It balances out 8 fixation.

@AskYatharth @QiaochuYuan @meekaale And so the chord for a 5, is to start with knowledge, use it to become powerful (8), in the service of others(2). 5-8-2. @anesmithbeck Here's a stab (pun intended)

The point of scapegoating is to rid the community of tension and create a new harmony in the aftermath. Nearly all modern scapegoating practices fall short of the end goal, because it is typically difficult to create the unity-in-expulsion required.

@anesmithbeck Instead the form we have is sharpened polarities. Those who denounce him are signalling that they are trying to form a coaltion of those who will expel the wrongdoer, hoping to cleanse the community of his malign influence. It's a disgust response.

@anesmithbeck But for those who have turned him into guru; their semidivine model that they imitate in order to gain his fullness of being, they will defend him because in so doing they are defending their own sense of selves. To reject him as a model would be a humiliation, a defeat.

@anesmithbeck And so they accuse his enemies of the very thing that animates their installation of him as model: envy. They crave his fullness of being. Since it so fills their mimetic entanglement with him, it's the only possibility they can see animating his accusers.

@anesmithbeck His defenders stay loyal due to little actual physical threat to them, only a felt sense of identity threat. His loyal disciples will form a new, semi-expelled minority and increasingly build their identity around grievance and enmity with the majority who have cast him out.

@anesmithbeck Thus the cycle repeats itself, never a complete scapegoating, but also at the same time the creation of a kind of martyr worshipping community around the incompletely expelled scapegoat. Nobody is happy.

@anesmithbeck Everyone stays mad and tense and hopes that the next scapegoat that comes along will be the one that is so horrible, so completely awful and degenerate, that everyone will be required to expel them and we will at last diffuse some tension.

@anesmithbeck But our consciences are grown too aware of the fact that no scapegoats are ever as guilty as required for the scapegoat mechanism to fully activate. Instead we await the day where we cease searching for scapegoats, that we work with the tension rather than trying to expel it.

@AbstractFairy Are you familiar with Spiral Dynamics and/or Integral?

@AbstractFairy So Kegan stages are a big deal in tpot, a metatheory of individual psychological evolution. More capacity, capable of coping with higher complexity. Spiral Dynamics is that, but for cultural evolutionary metatheory. Integral spans both, but let's stick to Spiral Dynamics (SD).

@AbstractFairy SD says: real evolution happens culturally. Higher levels are not more moral, although people within certain levels will invariably see their own level as the most moral and members of higher or lower tiers as immoral.

@AbstractFairy Noblesse Oblige arises within Blue/Traditional. It is a decent way of handling complexity in a pre-industrial, feudal world. But we don't live in that world any more, however much there are pockets of those who still adhere to (fundies) or long to retvrn to Blue.

@AbstractFairy But we've moved to Orange/Rational, and later into Green/Postmodern, our current cultural centre of gravity (~woke). To these, Noblesse Oblige looks primitive, naive, immoral, but for different reasons.

To Oranges, democrats, it looks like right of kings, despotic, unscientific.

@AbstractFairy For Greens, they see the hypocrisy of oppressing the peasants and holding yourself above them, like, bro, they wouldn't need help if you weren't crushing them. Also Greens still have a disavowed White Man's Burden, so they have to shit on earlier iterations of it.

The human individual is a mutation, an aberration, the nascent form of a new species, Homo Solus, the Alone Human.

We are not designed for this. Homo Sapiens functions as a collectivity; a hive mind.

The individual emerges from the collective, but most try to think vice versa.

@C6SI14 Interesting takeaway! The glory of Homo Solus is definitely specialization. The trap, however, is that the specialist can grow too detached from consensus reality and other people, which feels bad. Real "the difference between genius and madness is success" territory.

Human psychology is approximately infinitely malleable.

And that can feel terrifying, especially if you already feel unsafe.

Your sense of "I'm just the way I am, change is impossible" is a tiny void bubble you've made to cope with the terror of the infinite.
@itinerantfog can commend https://t.co/NssCmCul3E

That void bubble is commonly known as the self, or the ego, sometimes also the false self. It's formed out of contraction, tensioning, what gets called taṇhā, clinging. The clenched fist.

But clenching into this bubble shape feels bad, unpleasant, suffering, dukkha.

What do?

Western psychotherapeutic interventions typically focus on the story we tell ourselves about our void bubble, about maintaining its shape or subtly modifying it to be more stable, less grindy. The infinite is ignored, or outright shunned.

Spiritual paths would have you burst the bubble, or at least be able to drop it more or less at will (and fabricate others, skillfully). This is a big relief because holding that bubble is a drain, leaving abundant energy to move through the infinite, which is unutterable joy.
Because the sense of separateness we live with while identified with our bubbles is really shitty. Bad times. And you can just drop that entirely. It feels scary at first, but better and better.

People can have some pretty bad experiences around depersonalization/derealization or even psychosis as the centre drops away, which is why it's pretty important to be a part of a community of spiritual practice who know how to support you through that.

But a good set of grounding and stable relationships are about the same. I get to relativize the awe and terror of the infinite with "what can we feed our children today" and "fix this bug" as I live the absolute in all my corporeal, related, contingency.

But there's a lot of chatter about safety in the communities that intend to dissolve the bubble. Less talk about why we make these suffering bubbles in the first place. Was it always this way, or is it some kind of artefact of ~civilization? (Me: the latter, more another time.)

@cekaur Low dose 5-MeO-DMT is particularly promising in this regard. See https://t.co/zETdY6U2aI

Some stuff I wrote to another meditator in the next tweet:

@cekaur Most stuff you hear about 5-MeO-DMT dangers has to do with high doses, both the good and the bad. It's like riding a cosmic rocket into the void. So strong, so fast. Total dissolution. I have no experience with either cessation/stream-entry or nirodha samapatti as attained…

Most stuff you hear about 5-MeO-DMT dangers has to do with high doses, both the good and the bad. It's like riding a cosmic rocket into the void. So strong, so fast. Total dissolution. I have no experience with either cessation/stream-entry or nirodha samapatti as attained through meditation, but the described phenomenology lines up quite closely. "You" are "gone", and then you get to "come back into your body", seeing your faculties come back online. And like nirodha samapatti, you can be tremendously energized and refreshed afterwards.

But for people who are still very attached to a sense of self, having it ripped away with a high dose, trying to cling to earth from that rocket, is very unpleasant. I think that most experienced meditators will be able to let go into the experience pretty well. That's most of it. Surrender, and it's amazing. Cling, and it's hell.

But you were speaking of low doses, and they are an entirely different thing.

Here is the ~tpot-speak analogy I'm working with currently: 5-MeO is a resonant gong that entrains you to align with its vibrations. It rings a gong of wholeness, utter oneness, that invites all of your fractious parts into that wholeness. Your parts may resist this, which is why it's unpleasant for some people. The stronger the resistance, the more unpleasant. This substance is all about surrender.

And here's where we get to dose: a high dose is like being right next to a massive gong that's been struck by the world's strongest person. It utterly dominates your being, you have no choice but to literally vibrate with it. You do not have a choice, and if you have parts that are trying to say no, again, unpleasant, but the note is beautiful to surrender to.

But lower doses are much softer strikes to the gong, allowing you more agency in the process, not completely overwhelming your system. Many people report that low doses of 5-MeO-DMT produce anxiety, but my take on it is that it's merely revealing suppressed anxiety. 5-MeO takes away your ability to suppress, et voila, it "made" you anxious.

And this gets to the heart of the usefulness of low dose 5-MeO-DMT: these dynamics basically put a bright spotlight on whatever parts of you are fractured by overlaying a contrasting sense of wholeness on top of it. 

So from a therapeutic/growth orientation, this is actually tremendously useful material. I found out in an assembly, my face flushed as I was called up on stage, mortified to be outed as Math Smart. Berating myself for letting the teacher talk me into entering. Furious that I didn’t get advance notice to skip the assembly.

I spent high school desperately/futilely chasing the approval of popular kids, and this felt like a death blow. Just a nerd who will never be cool. Good at math == bad at people seemed like the truest equation in the universe. 

I felt all of that today and loved that desperately awkward teenager doing his absolute best while operating under many layers of delusion. There was never anything wrong with him.

We say "fight or flight" when it comes to Sympathetic Nervous System activation, but flight is what really gets us stuck.

We say "fight or flight" when it comes to Sympathetic Nervous System activation, but flight is what really gets us stuck.

Fight is a simple response with simple pros and cons. But you can't fight your environment, especially not while you're a child. You can't flee from it either, not physically, but anybody exposed to persisting threat, and especially where the persisting threat comes from their caretakers, is eventually overwhelmed by SNS activation energy with nothing to fight, and nowhere to flee.

"Gotta get out of here" says the self that also has no way out of here. But a way does open to flee: the flight within, the withdrawal from the world. Numbing. Dissociation. The Parasympathetic Nervous System cools the fires of nonstop SNS activation. You learn to fawn, or freeze.

Dissociation is the next logical step in flight that cannot flee. This is why the dissociated must learn how to not just not numb.

You must learn to fight.

@QiaochuYuan This is a novel theory that synthesizes a whole bunch I've been digesting, might be nothing to it.

This is a novel theory that synthesizes a whole bunch I've been digesting, might be nothing to it. 

Let's use a "steal from my parent" example. With guilt, the activation is anxious, might drive you to confess, attempt to return the thing surreptitiously to avoid being caught. There is a possible path to restitution. Rational weighing of possible actions. You want to make amends and might be able to.

With shame, I can never confess, never make amends, there is no path to restitution. Paralyzing. Hopeless. Defenseless.

Guilt is a mature, learned, anxious-to-please response. Shame is an immature, instinctual response.

@heynibras Have you heard of Christopher (Hareesh) Wallis? He's a scholar/practitioner of what he calls Nondual Shaiva Tantra, which died out in its home in Kashmir, but lives on, partially, in some other traditions, including hatha yoga.
